mnemonics.title: Dense = Array, Sparse = Linked
mnemonics.content: Think of it like a parking lot: A Dense lot (most spots taken) works well as an array because every space is used. A Sparse lot (mostly empty) is wasteful as an array; instead, just keep a Linked list of which spots are occupied. D-A, S-L: Dense-Array, Sparse-Linked.

mnemonics.title: CEN - Coefficient, Exponent, Next
mnemonics.content: Each node in a polynomial linked list has three fields: CEN = Coefficient, Exponent, Next pointer. Think 'CEN-tral node' to remember the three essential fields that make up each term in the polynomial linked list.

mnemonics.title: Compare-Act-Advance for Polynomial Addition
mnemonics.content: Polynomial addition follows the CAA pattern: Compare exponents of current nodes, Act (add coefficients if equal, or copy the larger), Advance the pointer(s). It is like merging two sorted playlists by release date: compare dates, pick the relevant song, move forward.

mnemonics.title: Degree = Highest Power
mnemonics.content: The degree of a polynomial is like the highest floor in a building. No matter how many floors there are below, the degree is determined only by the topmost one. In 4x^5 + 3x^2 + 1, the degree is 5 (the highest floor).

mnemonics.title: Term-Based Array: Coefficient + Exponent Pairs
mnemonics.content: Think of a term-based array like a phonebook: each entry has a pair of values (name and number). In polynomials, each entry is a pair of coefficient and exponent.

mnemonics.title: Linked List: Dynamic, Efficient, Flexible
mnemonics.content: A linked list is like a dynamic team: members can join or leave at any time, and the team size adjusts automatically. Linked lists are efficient for sparse polynomials, flexible for insertion and deletion, and dynamic for memory allocation.
